stories.title: Top US general says Ukraine's F-16s recently shot down their first Russian fighter jet, showing how far its air defense has come
stories.summary: Ukraine's F-16 fighter jets scored their first air-to-air kill by shooting down a Russian fighter jet, a top US general said, calling the engagement a sign of Ukraine's improving air defenses. US Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said during a congressional hearing on Tuesday that "recently we had the first air-to-air kill where a Ukrainian F-16 shot down a Russian fighter." He described it as evidence of Ukraine's growing air-defense capability. "I think their ability to scale their layered defense has come a long way over the last couple years with the help and assistance of many," he said at a Senate Appropriations Committee hearing.

stories.title: Tuscaloosa County teen Will Roberts dies after long battle with cancer
stories.summary: A Tuscaloosa County family that inspired thousands with their faith and resilience is mourning the loss of their son. Will Roberts has died after a long battle with cancer. His mother, Brittney Battles Roberts, shared the news in an emotional post, saying Will “rang his forever cancer-free bell” as he entered the gates of heaven. As the family grieves, they are asking for continued prayers in the difficult days ahead.

stories.title: LIV’s team event on brink of cancellation, per captain Martin Kaymer
stories.summary: LIV Golf's 2026 season finale in Michigan is "highly unlikely" to take place, according to Cleeks team captain Martin Kaymer, who estimates a mere 5% chance of the event occurring. This announcement comes as the league struggles to secure funding after losing financial backing from Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund. While the next three events in England, New Jersey, and Indianapolis are expected to proceed, the future of LIV Golf remains uncertain.

stories.title: July 24, 2026: Roman Andres Burruchaga vs Alexander Blockx: Set 2 Winner Tennis Prediction Market
stories.summary: Roman Andres Burruchaga vs Alexander Blockx: Set 2 Winner R. Andres Burruchaga 0 0 ¢ — 0 ¢ A. Blockx 0 LIVE 1D 1W MAX Select a contract Roman Andres Burruchaga 0¢ Alexander Blockx 0¢ Timeline Trading hours 24 hours a day, except Thursday 3AM-5AM ET Event day July 24, 2026 Contract resolves Determines the outcome of the contract Payout Usually within 1 hour of event resolution About Who will win Roman Andres Burruchaga vs Alexander Blockx Set 2 (Jul 24)? The following market refers to the Roman Andres Burruchaga vs Alexander Blockx professional tennis match in the 2026 ATP Estoril Quarterfinal.

stories.title: Aubrey Plaza Opens up About Pregnancy & Motherhood for the 1st Time
stories.summary: Aubrey Plaza Opens up About Pregnancy & Motherhood for the 1st Time Aubrey Plaza recently announced her pregnancy, revealing how “interesting” it is. She appeared on the “Smartness” podcast’s special bonus episode and opened up about motherhood. The 41-year-old said she was excited about becoming a first-time mom. Currently, she has been dating actor Christopher Abbott. Pregnant Aubrey Plaza reveals she ‘always wanted’ to be a mom in new podcast episode Pregnant Aubrey Plaza discussed being a first-time mom on the “Smartness” podcast, saying she “always wanted” to be one.

stories.title: France evacuates 20,000 as wildfires spread in south-west
stories.summary: Most of those evacuated were tourists from campsites around Arcachon Bay in France's Gironde region. About 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France and authorities are moving 8,800 others as wildfires spread in the Gironde region, the local prefect has said. Sophie Brocas told reporters 2,400 hectares (5,900 acres) of land had already been destroyed around the Bay of Arcachon. Many of those who had to leave the area overnight were visitors to local campsites on the Lège-Cap-Ferret peninsula and the latest evacuations also involved campsites as a precaution. Wildfires have also broken out in Spain and Italy in recent days.

stories.title: Richards hopeful of completing medal set in Glasgow
stories.summary: Double Olympic champion Matt Richards is hoping to claim Commonwealth gold and complete his major medal collection. The 23-year-old swimmer won Olympic golds in 2021 and 2024 and has multiple world and European titles to his name. But four fourth-place finishes in Birmingham in 2022 has left a hole in an otherwise glittering trophy cabinet. "It would mean the world," said Richards, of winning gold in Glasgow 2026. "Every time I've ever stood at the top of the podium I've heard the British national anthem, which is fantastic and amazing and every time it's special. "But I've never been able to stand at the top of the podium and hear the Welsh anthem and that's something important to me.

stories.title: Business rate cut not enough, says Michelin-star chef
stories.summary: Alex Claridge, owner of The Wilderness in Birmingham, said the proposal did not go far enough. For Michelin-starred chef Alex Claridge, running an independent restaurant has become a difficult task. Despite the acclaim his Birmingham restaurant The Wilderness commands, Claridge says the reality behind the kitchen doors is one of soaring costs, shrinking margins and constant uncertainty. It's a picture he believes is shared by independent hospitality businesses across the country.

stories.title: Johnson-Thompson and Asher-Smith out of Commonwealths
stories.summary: Olympic silver medallist Katarina Johnson-Thompson pulls out of the Commonwealth Games because of injury. Olympic silver medallist Katarina Johnson-Thompson has pulled out of the Commonwealth Games because of injury. Dina Asher-Smith and 400m Olympic silver medalist Matt Hudson-Smith, who were both set to run relays for Team England in Glasgow, have also been replaced, as has Asher-Smith's sprint team-mate Daryll Neita and 400m runner Amber Anning. Johnson-Thompson, 33, was due to compete in her third Commonwealth Games heptathlon, having won gold on the Gold Coast in 2018 and at Birmingham in 2022.

stories.title: Ex-Coronation Street actor to receive 'substantial' libel damages from the Sun publisher
stories.summary: Qasim Akhtar is to receive "substantial" damages to settle a High Court libel claim from a story last eyar. Former Coronation Street actor Qasim Akhtar is to receive "substantial" damages from the Sun's publisher, to settle a High Court libel claim. Akhtar who played Zeedan Nazir in the ITV soap from 2014 to 2018 and then 2021 to 2023, sued News Group Newspapers (NGN) earlier this year. A story in the Sun had falsely alleged Akhtar had "backed" and "teamed up with" Islamic cleric Uthman ibn Farooq, was associated with extremism, radicalisation and violence, and had "moved to Pakistan" and "trained with guns".

stories.title: Illegal waste investigation sees £275k forfeited
stories.summary: The investigation spans Herefordshire, Worcestershire and Shropshire, police say. Three businesses that are being investigated by police who are looking into illegal waste activity have forfeited a total of more than £275,000. West Mercia Police said additional crimes had been identified in its investigation across Herefordshire, Worcestershire and Shropshire. The force added that more assets were under review and a money laundering investigation was still active. It comes after two men were arrested at an address in Drakes Broughton, Worcestershire, in January. Six firearms were seized at that location, along with about £18,000 in cash.

stories.title: Poorest children in England 40% more likely to be hospitalised for lung infections
stories.summary: Children living in the poorest areas across England are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for respiratory infections, an analysis of NHS England data shows, with experts raising the alarm on the impact of air pollution on young people’s health. The analysis, based on data obtained via freedom of information requests by the charity Impact on Urban Health, revealed 1,941 out of every 100,000 children living in the most deprived fifth of England were admitted to hospital for respiratory illnesses in 2024-25.

stories.title: England U16 player fight was resolved quickly - FA
stories.summary: The FA says a fight between two players at an England Under-16s camp was caused by an "internal matter". A fight between England Under-16 players at a camp in Turkey was caused by an "internal matter" which was "quickly resolved", says the FA. An online clip from the camp in late-2025 shows two players fighting in a hotel room, while several others film and encourage them. The FA said the issue has been settled and no further action will be taken. An FA spokesperson said: "We were made aware of an internal matter during an Under-16s camp in October 2025.

stories.title: I wanted a clock that never needed setting. Things escalated.
stories.summary: Sure, maybe your clock has a backup battery, but mine has a deployment pipeline! Let’s be clock bros Sure, maybe your clock has a backup battery, but mine has a deployment pipeline! Rapid prototyping! Credit: Lee Hutchinson Rapid prototyping! Credit: Lee Hutchinson I wanted a clock that, annoyingly, didn’t seem to exist. Since childhood, my bedside clocks have been a series of red, seven-segment LED clock-radio specials from Walmart or Target. They are invariably cheap, simple, and long-lived—but they require manual intervention at the start and end of Daylight Saving Time and whenever the power flickers.

stories.title: ‘Why do I feel crappy?’: women are learning about their luteal phases - and Maha is weaponizing it
stories.summary: E mily Rae Detrick, a health coach, wants women to be more in touch with their menstrual cycle in its entirety. This, she said on an October episode of Culture Apothecary with Alex Clark, a podcast popular among “Make America Healthy Again” (Maha) adherents, is “a way that you honor God and the way we were created”. It also serves her self-assigned mission to help “make America fertile again”. In the episode and on social media, Detrick speaks at length about the luteal phase, the 14-ish days between ovulation and menstruation when women are more likely to experience irritability, bloating and food cravings.

stories.title: Gary Lineker among millionaires asking Andy Burnham to tax them more
stories.summary: A group including the former England footballer write to the new PM telling him "we can afford it". Millionaires including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed more. In an open letter, 120 well-off Britons told Burnham: "We can afford it. We're not talking about higher taxes on those who get up and go to work for their income every day, but on the very richest whose income is derived from the wealth they hold." Organised by Patriotic Millionaires, the letter said it would lead to a more equal society, and urged a "devolution of wealth and power from the very richest".

stories.title: White House report says Trump can usher in a "new golden age" of science
stories.summary: The resulting report is a near-random mixture of real problems and grievances. Gold-plated? It's a golden age, of sorts, in the White House at least. Credit: Bloomberg On Tuesday, the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) released a report entitled “Science: A New Golden Age,” in which it lays out how it has viewed science, found it lacking, and believes the Trump administration is in the perfect position to fix things. It’s a bit unexpected coming from an administration that has been proposing crippling funding cuts to research and trying to enable political appointees to terminate grants awarded based on scientific merit.

stories.title: The right-wing boomers protesting data centers have a lot in common with the left
stories.summary: On a gray, humid Saturday morning in central Florida, a little under a dozen people gathered outside the Spring Hill Branch Library to protest the construction of a hyperscale data center in their community. There was no immediate threat — the Hernando County commission had unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on such developments in June — but the organizers weren’t satisfied. A temporary pause wouldn’t be enough. They wanted a ban. “We are going to fight, fight, fight,” said a 71-year-old woman named Carol, who was manning a table with flyers and a petition the group, No Data Centers Hernando County, planned on presenting to the local government.

stories.title: Islanders asked to 'track' not 'trample' Asian hornets
stories.summary: More than 200 Asian hornet queens have been caught and nine nests destroyed so far this year. People in Guernsey are being asked to "track" not "trample" Asian hornets in an effort to protect public health and the island's biodiversity. Asian hornets spread through Europe after arriving in southern France in 2004, having reportedly hitched a ride in a consignment of pottery from Asia. They first appeared in Guernsey in 2017 and the States said the non-native, invasive species poses a major threat to the island's biodiversity and honeybees, which make up 39% of its diet on average.

stories.title: Google hit with $1 billion fine for breaking EU antitrust rules
stories.summary: The European Union has fined Google’s parent company Alphabet €890 million (about $1 billion) for two separate violations of the bloc’s Digital Markets Act (DMA). One penalty is for giving its own products preferential treatment in search results, while the other is for blocking Android developers from sending users to alternate payment options. A €460 million fine has been issued to Google for giving preferential treatment to its own Shopping, Hotels, and Flights services in Google Search results. The second €430 million penalty is for Play Store rules preventing developers from freely steering consumers to alternative payment systems that may be cheaper.

stories.title: Zelensky’s Dizzying U-Turn Is the Latest Zigzag in a Presidency Full of Them
stories.summary: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine has pivoted from unpopular moves before, but a recent decision about military leadership and strategy could weaken his position.

stories.title: Global Oil Price Nears $100 a Barrel as Conflict With Iran Deepens
stories.summary: The Houthis claimed to have attacked two ships in the Red Sea, and Iran said a third caught fire near the Strait of Hormuz.

stories.title: Nuclear Energy Revival Puts Westinghouse in Prime Position
stories.summary: The company, which filed for bankruptcy protection in 2017, stands to benefit from growing support for nuclear power and President Trump’s deal with Saudi Arabia.

stories.title: U.S. Military Presence in Jordan Makes It Iranian Target
stories.summary: Relying heavily on U.S. aid and protection, Jordan is unlikely to restrict the American military presence there, despite escalating Iranian airstrikes.

stories.title: South Korea woman acquitted of murdering newborn after hospital delivery
stories.summary: The woman, who had wanted to end her pregnancy at 36 weeks, gave birth to a baby who doctors later killed. A South Korean court has acquitted a woman previously convicted of murdering a baby she delivered in hospital - the latest twist in a case that has gripped the country and fuelled debate about its murky abortion laws. The woman had wanted to terminate her pregnancy at 36 weeks, but prosecutors said the baby was born alive and later killed. The woman, identified only by her surname Kwon, was convicted of murder alongside two doctors in March. She was given a three-year suspended jail sentence.

stories.title: A Peptide Showdown: F.D.A. Scientists May Clash With R.F.K.’s Agenda
stories.summary: Agency scientists are advising against allowing widespread use of several peptide injections. Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the health secretary, has said people should be free to experiment.

stories.title: 'Life-changing' MS drug to help people walk now on NHS in England
stories.summary: Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier to walk, with up to 5,000 eligible. The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England. Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently. Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a "huge difference". Up to 5,000 people in England could benefit each year. The drug is already available elsewhere in the UK. Multiple sclerosis is caused by the immune system attacking the protective covering around nerve cells - known as myelin.

US Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said during a congressional hearing on Tuesday that "recently we had the first air-to-air kill where a Ukrainian F-16 shot down a Russian fighter." He described it as evidence of Ukraine's growing air-defense capability. "I think their ability to scale their layered defense has come a long way over the last couple years with the help and assistance of many," he said at a Senate Appropriations Committee hearing.

While the next three events in England, New Jersey, and Indianapolis are expected to proceed, the future of LIV Golf remains uncertain.
